petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented calligrapher seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. The petitioner has been residing in the United States since January 2001, and filed the petition on October 8, 2004. In support of the petition, the petitioner initially submitted nine photographs of what is alleged to be his calligraphy work.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a Certificate of Honor dated January 1999 with an accompanying English language translation indicating that he received a 'first grade award of national calligraphy contest.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this evidence insufficient because the record contained no evidence of publicity surrounding this contest or evidence showing that the petitioner's award enjoys a significant level of recognition. Simply receiving an honor certificate with the word 'national' in the title does not satisfy this very restrictive criterion. The petitioner must provide evidence showing that his award enjoys significant national or international stature, and the record contained no documentation from the awarding entity or print media to establish that the Certificate of Honor is a nationally recognized artistic award. Additionally, the translation accompanying the Certificate of Honor was not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires foreign language documents to be accompanied by a full English language translation certified as complete and accurate by a translator competent to translate from the foreign language into English.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a photocopy of the cover of a publication entitled 'International Hard Pen Calligrapher Dictionary' of which he claims he is an author.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this evidence insufficient because the record contained no evidence showing the part of this publication that the petitioner authored or quantitative evidence of the publication's significant national or international readership. Nor was there supporting evidence indicating that the petitioner's published material (if any) is viewed throughout his field as significantly influential.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner re-submitted the nine photographs of what is alleged to be his calligraphy. On appeal, the petitioner added captions to these photographs indicating the dollar amount and date of sale of each work, claiming that his 'calligraphy works are sold at high prices.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this evidence insufficient because the record contained no evidence to support the petitioner's assertions regarding the amounts he earned from the sale of his calligraphy. Going on record without supporting documentary evidence is not sufficient for purposes of meeting the burden of proof in these proceedings. There was no evidence showing that the petitioner's compensation is significantly higher than that of other professional calligraphers.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to demonstrate that he meets at least three of the criteria required to establish sustained national or international acclaim. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself to such an extent that he may be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. Beyond the director's decision, the AAO also noted that the record contained no evidence required under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) showing that the alien is coming to the United States to continue work in the area of expertise, such as letters from prospective employers, prearranged commitments, or a statement detailing plans to continue his work. Each reason was considered an independent and alternative basis for denial.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The translation accompanying the petitioner's Certificate of Honor was not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires foreign language documents to be accompanied by a certified English translation. Additionally, the petitioner was self-represented, and the AAO identified a procedural deficiency beyond the director's decision: the record contained no evidence required under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) establishing that the alien is coming to the United States to continue work in the area of expertise.
